str:Code.com:Description: Crude Oil (petroleum), Price index, 2016 = 100, simple average of three spot prices; Dated Brent, West Texas Intermediate, and the Dubai Fateh. This indicator appears in other datasets published by the IMF. While every effort is made to ensure that estimates for the same indicator are consistent across different datasets, differences can occur due to differences in compilation methodology, reference year, splicing series to preserve time-series continuity, and when the data were collected, processed, and published. A commodity price index is a measurement tool that tracks the average price changes of a basket of commodities over time. These commodities can include various raw materials or primary agricultural products A statistical measure that expresses the relative change in a variable over time.

str:Code.com:Name: Gross capital formation, Percent of GDP
str:Code.com:Description: Acquisitions less disposals of produced assets for purposes of fixed capital formation, inventories or valuables. Value expressed as a percent of the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) of a country. A ratio expressed as a fraction of 100.

str:Code.com:Name: Vegetable oil, Commodity price index
str:Code.com:Description: Vegetable Oil Index, 2016 = 100, includes Soybean, Soybean Meal, Soybean Oil, Coconut Oil, Palm Oil, Sunflower Oil and Olive Oil Price Indices. This indicator appears in other datasets published by the IMF. While every effort is made to ensure that estimates for the same indicator are consistent across different datasets, differences can occur due to differences in compilation methodology, reference year, splicing series to preserve time-series continuity, and when the data were collected, processed, and published. A commodity price index is a measurement tool that tracks the average price changes of a basket of commodities over time. These commodities can include various raw materials or primary agricultural products A statistical measure that expresses the relative change in a variable over time.

str:Code.com:Name: Bank stock index, 12-month rolling beta
str:Code.com:Description: A financial index that tracks the performance of a basket of stocks issued by banking institutions. This index provides insights into the health and performance of the banking sector, reflecting changes in stock prices of banks in response to economic, regulatory, and market conditions. A 12-month rolling beta is a statistical measure that shows the volatility of an investment compared to a benchmark index (like the S&P 500) over a 12-month period. This calculation is done continuously, with each new month's data replacing the oldest month in the calculation, creating a "rolling" effect. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of the standardized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Corporate bond yield spread
str:Code.com:Description: The difference in yield between corporate bonds and government bonds of similar maturity, reflecting credit risk. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Flexible Exchange Market Pressure Index
str:Code.com:Description: Measures the pressure on a country's exchange rate in a flexible exchange rate system, taking into account factors such as changes in exchange rates, interest rates, and international reserves. It helps in identifying periods of excessive upward or downward pressure on the currency, indicating potential stability or vulnerability in the foreign exchange market. Calculated as one unit equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: EMBI Global - stripped spread
str:Code.com:Description: The spread on emerging market bonds, adjusted for risk by stripping out risk-free components. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial stress indicators
str:Code.com:Description: Quantitative measures that signal the level of stress in the financial system. These indicators can include volatility in stock and bond markets, widening credit spreads, and increased interbank lending rates. They are used to identify periods of financial turbulence that could impact economic stability and inform policy responses. Calculated as the sum of standardized subcomponents.

str:Code.com:Name: Inverted term spread
str:Code.com:Description: A situation where short-term interest rates are higher than long-term interest rates, often seen as a predictor of economic recession. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Emerging market stock returns
str:Code.com:Description: The returns on stocks from emerging markets. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Real effective exchange rate (REER), 6-month rolling monthly squared change, major trade partner weighted, adjusted by relative consumper prices.
str:Code.com:Description: A real effective exchange rate (REER) is a measure of the value of a currency against a weighted average of several foreign currencies divided by a price deflator or index of costs. The weights are derived by taking a three-year average of the trade partners' combined value of imports and exports with the focal country as a share of the focal countries' total combined value of imports and exports. An increase in REER implies that exports become more expensive and imports become cheaper; therefore, an increase indicates a loss in trade competitiveness. Measures the volatility of a data series by calculating the squared difference between each month's value and the previous month's value, averaged over a rolling 6-month window. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of the standardized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Stock returns, 6-month rolling monthly squared change
str:Code.com:Description: Measures the profit or loss generated on an investment in a stock over a specific period. Stock returns can be calculated based on price appreciation (capital gains) and any dividends received, offering investors a comprehensive view of the performance and profitability of their stock investments. Measures the volatility of a data series by calculating the squared difference between each month's value and the previous month's value, averaged over a rolling 6-month window. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of the standardized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Financial price index
str:Code.com:Description: A composite index that tracks the movement of financial asset prices, such as stocks, bonds, and other securities. It provides insights into the overall performance of financial markets and can signal shifts in investor sentiment, economic expectations, and market conditions. Calculated as one unit equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.

str:Code.com:Name: Monthly stock returns (measured as declines), Measured as declines
str:Code.com:Description: The performance of stocks on a monthly basis, specifically measured by declines in value. One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable. This indicates that the unit of measurement for this variable represents the number or magnitude of declines observed in a particular phenomenon. For example, it might measure the number of job losses in a particular industry or the decline in biodiversity in a specific region.

str:Code.com:Name: Treasury-EuroDollar spread (TED) or interbank spread
str:Code.com:Description: The difference between the interest rates on short-term U.S. government debt (Treasury bills) and Eurodollar deposits (LIBOR). One unit is equivalent to one standard deviation of standarized variable.
